chunk_summary: Focuses on feeding efficiency, safety, and caregiver techniques for infants with cleft palate, emphasizing growth velocity, feeding observation, and pre-surgical nutritional readiness.
learning_objectives: Understand feeding challenges in infants with cleft palate, including inefficient sucking and nasal regurgitation.
Learn to assess feeding efficiency, safety, and caregiver techniques for optimal growth and nutrition.
Identify and address barriers to oral feeding, including device, skill, or socioeconomic factors.
Recognize the importance of multidisciplinary care and pre-surgical nutritional goals.
key_facts: Infants with cleft palate often experience inefficient sucking and nasal regurgitation.
Prolonged feeds increase energy expenditure and reduce net intake; feeding duration should be limited.
Growth velocity is a key outcome for assessing feeding adequacy.
Specialty feeding devices (e.g., squeezable bottles) and caregiver coaching improve feeding safety.
Pre-surgical planning requires achieving a minimum weight and stable nutrition.
Early referral to SLP and cleft clinics supports safe swallowing and timely surgery.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
tags_mesh: Feeding and Eating Disorders of Childhood
reference_quote: Infants with cleft palate often have inefficient sucking and nasal regurgitation, so dietary assessment must focus on feeding efficiency, safety, and caregiver technique rather than food choice alone. (Source: MedlinePlus, https://medlineplus.gov/cleftlipandpalate.html)

subtopic: CONDITION-SPECIFIC FEEDING RISK ASSESSMENT AND DIET MODIFICATIONS
sub_subtopic: CLEFT PALATE FEEDING EFFICIENCY SAFETY AND SURGICAL READINESS
